title: Week 2 recap Pricing
body:
Week 2 recap – Pricing

 • Objective is to determine forward or futures price
   • We start with a forward contact as there is no daily settlement.
   • We then consider a futures contract and show that when the interest rate is constant, futures
    price is equal to forward price.

 • Arbitrage pricing strategy
   • We price derivatives by constructing replicating portfolios.

 • Assumptions:
   • Complete markets
   • No transaction costs
   • Same constant interest rate (continuously compounded) for everybody
   • No taxes
   • Arbitrage-free prices
   • Ignore the cost of short-selling
                                                                                                     1
 Arbitrage pricing of forwards on non-dividend-paying stock

 • To find the arbitrage-free price we want to replicate the cash flows of a long forward
  contract:
   • It costs nothing to enter the contract since 𝑓! =0
   • At time T, we pay 𝐹! and receive the underlying (a non-dividend paying stock) worth 𝑆"




 • We can replicate 𝑆! by buying the underlying today, 𝑆"

 • We can replicate– 𝐹" at time T by borrowing the present value of 𝐹" today, 𝐹" 𝑒 #$!




                                                                                              2
 Arbitrage pricing of forwards

 •We have constructed our replicating portfolio so that the cash flows at
  time T are equal
 •In an arbitrage-free market, this implies that the cash flows at time 0
  are also equal:




 • This relationship is known as a cost of carry – the forward price is
  equal to the cost of carrying the underlying asset from today to
  maturity. In this case, the underlying asset is the non-dividend-paying
  stock.                                                                    3
 Arbitrage pricing of forwards – Alternative replicating portfolio

 • We will construct an alternative replicating portfolio to match the cash flow of
     the forward contract at 𝑡! .
      • Take a long position in a forward contract: −𝑓!
      • Borrow 𝑆! and buy stock - 𝑆!
      • Cash flows at 𝑡! : 𝑆! - 𝑆! = 𝑓! = 0
 • Arbitrage free implies that the cash flow of the replicating portfolio at T is
     equal to the cash flow of the forward contract.
                                                 #"
      • Cash flows at T: 𝑆" − 𝐹! = 𝑆" + 𝑆! 𝑒
      • 𝐻𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒: 𝐹! = 𝑆! 𝑒 #"
 • The forward price is equal to the cost of carrying stock from today to
     maturity


 •

                                                                                      4
                                       '(
What if: 𝐹& (Quoted) > 𝑆& 𝑒                (Arbitrage-free)

                                #"
 • Suppose 𝐹! (Quoted) > 𝑆! 𝑒        (Arbitrage-free)
   • The forward price is quoted “too high” – sell forward, borrow and buy underlying

 • Initial time cash flows?

 • Time T cash flows?

 • Arbitrage profit: 𝐹! 𝑒 $#" − 𝑆! > 0: Positive cash flow at time 0 …. J

 • This trade is known as cash-and-carry arbitrage.




                                                                                        5
 Reverse cash-and-carry arbitrage

                                 #"
 • What if 𝐹! (quoted) < 𝑆! 𝑒
   • The forward price is “too low” – buy forward, short selling underlying, and
    lend money out

 • Cash flows?
                                 $#"
 • Arbitrage profit: 𝑆! − 𝐹! 𝑒         > 0: positive cash flow at time 0




                                                                                   6
 Forward contracts on other assets

 • Consider a forward contract on a stock that pays a dividend I’ at time t between time 0
  and T.
   • The arbitrage-free forward price is 𝐹" = (𝑆" −𝐼) 𝑒 $!
 • Consider a forward contract on stock indices that pay continuously compounding
  dividend yield q,
   • The arbitrage-free forward price is 𝐹" = 𝑆" 𝑒 ($#')!

 • Consider a forward contract on currency that pays a continuously compounding interest
  rate, 𝑟)
   • The arbitrage-free forward price is 𝐹" = 𝑆" 𝑒 ($#$! )!

 • Consider a forward contract on investment commodity
   • The arbitrage-free forward price is 𝐹" = 𝑆" 𝑒 ($*+)! 𝑜𝑟 𝐹" = (𝑆" +𝑈)𝑒 $!
 • Consider a forward contract on consumption commodity
   • The arbitrage-free forward price is 𝐹" = 𝑆" 𝑒 ($*+#,)! 𝑜𝑟 𝐹" = (𝑆" +𝑈) 𝑒 ($#,)!
                                                                                             7
 Example 5.6

 • Suppose that the 2-year interest rates in Australia and the United States are 5% and
  7%, respectively, and the spot exchange rate between the Australian dollar (AUD) and
  the US dollar (USD) is 0.6200 USD per AUD. When a 2-year forward exchange rate
  is quoted as 0.6300, what arbitrage opportunity does this create?
   • No arbitrage implies that 𝐹! = 0.62 𝑒 "%$%% ∗' = 0.6453
   • The quoted price of 0.63 implies that the forward contract is underpriced.
   • Today - take a long position in the forward contract to buy 1 unit of AUD, short selling
     𝑒 $(!) units of AUD (borrow AUD and convert it to USD) and receive USD 0.62* 𝑒 $(!)
     and then invest the USD at 7% for 2 years.
   • At T: from the forward contract, pay the quoted forward price USD 630 per AUD to buy 1
     unit of AUD, from the underlying (AUD) market, pay AUD back (𝑒 $(!) ∗ 𝑒 (!) , principle
     plus interest) and receive investment of USD 0.62*𝑒 (($(!))
   • Arbitrage profit = 0.62*𝑒 (($(!)) - 0.63 = 0.0153 for one forward contract.
   • Textbook: arbitrage profit is 16.91 for 1000𝑒 (!) forward contracts.
                                                                                                8
 Pricing of futures v.s. forwards

 • If the interest rate is constant, the forward price and futures price are equal

 • What if interest rates change over time?
   • When interest rate is positively (negatively) correlated to underlying asset
     price, future price will be higher (lower) than forward price.



 • The holder of a short position in a futures contract can choose when to make
  delivery

 • When c>y, futures price increases with T. Hence, the seller of the futures
  contract will made delivery as early as possible.

 • 5.12, 5.14, 5.27, 5.30, 5.31
